original_abstract:
This paper provides a formal theoretical framework for data validation, defining it as the process of determining a dataset’s fitness for purpose based on prior expectations. By modeling datasets as collections of key-value pairs and validation as surjective Boolean functions, the authors move beyond informal heuristics toward a rigorous logical foundation. This formalization enables a systemic classification of validation rules based on the complexity of the information required for their evaluation—ranging from simple single-variable checks to complex multi-unit, longitudinal, and cross-type assertions. 

The authors further explore the mathematical properties of validation rule sets, identifying critical issues that arise when multiple rules are combined, such as logical contradictions, partial inconsistencies, and various forms of redundancy. These complexities can lead to maintenance challenges and interfere with automated error localization. By framing data validation within the context of first-order logic and optimization, the paper demonstrates how formal methods, including Mixed Integer Programming, can be employed to simplify rule sets and improve the reliability of data cleaning pipelines in fields such as official statistics and data science.
humanized_abstract:
Abstract Validity of data is often regarded as being valid, if it fits to the preconceived ideas that one has of the data. The data validation research presented in this paper forms the formal basis for these notions. Here data validation is formally defined as testing the validity of a data set based on certain validation rules, with data sets and validation rules being transformed into formal model elements that can be analyzed logically, the data sets being modelled as a multiset of key–value pairs, the validation rules being modeled as surjective Boolean functions, which leads to a systematic categorization of data validation rules into certain complexity classes according to the amount of information that have to be known about the data to validate or reject each single data item. These complexity classes range from simple one-dimensional validation rules per key for a single data item, up to higher dimensional data validation, including multi-data item, and time-related as well as cross-class validation rules.

In our previous paper, we studied the mathematical structure of a validation rule set. In this work, we extend this research. By studying the problems arising from combining validation rules — such as the appearance of logical conflicts, partial inconsistency, and different forms of redundancy — we identify a number of challenges which arise when more than one validation rule is used. We show that solving these challenges makes the corresponding process of error localisation of data cleaning more difficult. Using first-order logic and optimization, and applying formal methods such as Mixed Integer Programming, we demonstrate how it is possible to obtain significantly simplified validation rule sets and, thus, improve data cleaning processes in applications such as official statistics and data science.
